response_raw:
{
    "mode": "human",
    "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
    "opinion": "As a man from Rome with decades of experience, I've always been practical in my approach. Social media has changed things fast for someone like me who didn't grow up with it, but now that we're using platforms occasionally – maybe once or twice a month at most – seeing all sorts of fake stuff makes you feel confused and sometimes sad. My depression means I'm extra cautious about misinformation; there's no fun in dealing with confusing lies online when life is already tricky.\n\nI believe the best way to handle this is by being careful, not just clicking on everything blindly like some younger people do because they're more curious. My mechanic background taught me that details matter and errors can cause problems – so I try to read things multiple times before believing them. Also, since I'm mostly single now with one child who loves cinema but we still go to church sometimes, my family isn't the main reason for social media use.\n\nPerhaps other methods exist too? Maybe official sources or groups that vet content. But personally, I stick to fact-checking when possible and ignore anything suspicious unless it's really important – like safety advice online during times when you need help.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
As a man from Rome with decades of experience, I've always been practical in my approach. Social media has changed things fast for someone like me who didn't grow up with it, but now that we're using platforms occasionally – maybe once or twice a month at most – seeing all sorts of fake stuff makes you feel confused and sometimes sad. My depression means I'm extra cautious about misinformation; there's no fun in dealing with confusing lies online when life is already tricky.

I believe the best way to handle this is by being careful, not just clicking on everything blindly like some younger people do because they're more curious. My mechanic background taught me that details matter and errors can cause problems – so I try to read things multiple times before believing them. Also, since I'm mostly single now with one child who loves cinema but we still go to church sometimes, my family isn't the main reason for social media use.

Perhaps other methods exist too? Maybe official sources or groups that vet content. But personally, I stick to fact-checking when possible and ignore anything suspicious unless it's really important – like safety advice online during times when you need help.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Step-by-step derivation: 1) Considering the persona traits (age 65, depressed, native Italian), the approach should be cautious and practical. 2) High conscientiousness suggests following rules or careful verification processes is key for managing fake content effectively while mitigating depression through selective engagement.
